title: Someone else's words
checkLabels.t4: It is clear who said it
why.empty: The editor is empty. You deleted the page instead of setting the quote apart. Bring the code back.
why.not-closed: A tag is open and never closed.
hint2.not-closed:
closes. And the

inside it closes too.
hint3.not-closed:
Whatever you opened last, you close first:
→

→

→
.
why.no-quote: The quote is not inside a <blockquote> yet. Quotation marks are just characters — the computer does not know these words are not yours. The tag says so.
hint2.no-quote: Wrap the borrowed words. Not the whole sentence — only what your friend actually said.
hint3.no-quote:
I did not say this.

— there is still a paragraph inside the quote. A quote is a block, not a word.
hint4.no-quote: Take "This is the best bike I have ever ridden" out of the sentence and put it in a <blockquote> below it.
why.kept-marks: The quotation marks are still there. Now you have both a tag and marks — you said the same thing twice, in two different ways. The tag already claims the words are someone else's. The marks were the old way, before you had a tag.
hint2.kept-marks: Look at what sits inside the <blockquote>. Is there anything there besides the words themselves?
hint3.kept-marks: The browser sets the quote apart with an indent on its own. It does not need your marks to show it.
hint4.kept-marks: Remove the two quotation marks around the quote. Only the text stays.
why.lost-attribution: Who said it is gone. A quote with no source is just a sentence hanging there. "A friend of mine said" must stay — that part is yours, not theirs.
hint2.lost-attribution:
The sentence splits in two: your part stays in the

, their part goes into the

.
why.lost-h1: The heading is gone. The task is to set the quote apart, not to delete things.
blocks.0.text: Someone said something about your bike and you want to quote them. You wrote it in quotation marks, inside a sentence. It works.
blocks.1.text: But quotation marks are just characters
blocks.3.text: <blockquote> says: these words are not mine. The browser sets it apart with an indent — but more importantly, the code now says so. And once the tag says it, the marks are redundant: do not say the same thing twice.
blocks.4.text: There is an inline version too — <q> — for a short quote in the middle of a sentence. It adds the quotation marks itself, and the right ones for the page's language at that.
blocks.6.text: Google tells a quote apart from your own text — and does not penalise you for copying someone else's. And if you ever want to pull every quote out of your site, you will need the tag, not the marks. A quotation mark turns up in the middle of words too; a tag does not.
blocks.7.text: Both take a cite attribute with an address — where the quote is from. It is not visible on screen, but it is there for whoever looks for the source.
